topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with an irritating monotony. I stared at the schematic spread across the table, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. It was the same circuit diagram, the same bizarre, interlocking logic gates, the same baffling theoretical framework that I had spent the last three weeks wrestling with. Then I saw it—a notation in the margin, a specific calibration sequence I had only just formulated an hour ago. A cold, sharp feeling settled in my chest, tightening my throat. I looked up, scanning the room, and my eyes locked onto another figure hunched over an identical blueprint, their concentration absolute, their posture mirroring mine with unnerving precision. A slow, deliberate movement pulled my hand away from my keyboard. I stood up, the scrape of my chair echoing too loudly in the silence, and walked toward them, my steps measured and brittle. "What have you been doing?" My voice was flat, devoid of any warmth.

[story 2]

My stomach clenched into a knot of pure resentment. I traced the lines on the digital rendering, feeling a surge of bitter frustration that burned behind my eyes. Every line, every calculated angle, felt like a deliberate mockery. I had poured sleepless nights into this design, and now to find it duplicated, executed by someone else—it was an insult layered upon an insult. I leaned back, letting out a slow, ragged breath that tasted like ash. Across the room, a shadow moved, and I saw the familiar silhouette. They were meticulously adjusting the same parameters on their console. A low growl escaped my lips, a sound more animal than human. I pushed my chair back with unnecessary force, the sound grating against the floor. I didn't say anything, just fixed them with a stare so intense it felt like physical pressure, daring them to acknowledge the shared, infuriating truth.

[story 3]

The air in the workshop felt thick, suffocating. I picked up the physical prototype, turning it over in my hands. It was flawless, undeniably mine, yet the knowledge that someone else had constructed this exact mechanism, this very configuration of components, made my skin crawl. A deep, simmering irritation began to build, a slow burn that radiated from my core. I paced the perimeter of the workspace, my movements jerky and agitated. When I finally spotted them—standing by the workbench, their expression one of intense focus—I stopped dead. I crossed my arms tightly across my chest, pulling my shoulders up toward my ears. "You," I managed, the word clipped and sharp. The tone I used was carefully constructed, laced with a profound sense of being undermined, a quiet declaration that this shared endeavor was now tainted by their presence.
